comment.body:
veta is vega decay.
long veta = dealer is longer vega by the time until it reaches zero exposure buying back less and less vega,
short veta = opposite.

why? dealers hedge vol exposure too, but not in every timeframe, and not always... its the effect that matters.
vega needs 4 weeks or more in terms of maturity to have an effect on the dealer flow.
but look...
vega can be hedged only by options, bcs futures have no vega..
but it is vanna that matters the most from vega as its first derivative.

comment.body: RE: AAPL. I don't know if i got lucky, as i'm really just starting out, but i read a bunch of research papers on straddles and identifying criteria for  it.  I was able to pull GARCH Rank, IV Rank and both were super low...i then looked at the implied move buy the straddle price and compared that to the current Keltner Channel.  The implied move was within the channel, so i pulled the trigger and its been successful.  Overall, i've been trying to refine my volatility compression strategy.  that's at least what i used - but i'd love to get Alma's feedback.

comment.body: Would just use Bitcoin for speculation, not as a store of value or means of payment. It has and will always require electrical power to work. Just think what will happen during floods as the recent ones in Florida - only physical cash (or silver/gold) accepted for more than a week. Also, in modern warfare, the first thing your enemy will try to destroy is your infrastructure, power grids etc. No more bitcoin without power.

comment.body: i'll take an OK!  Apologies - i did not mean to imply that the analysis caused the rip, the original question - i thought - was related to identifying the consolidation before the "rip".  From what i have been reading, those indicators are a few that indicate a potential volatility compression.  And to your earlier question about other TA indicators - when i entered the position AAPL was sitting on the base line of the ichimoku and running into the green cloud, and the 8 ema was just crossing over the 21 ema.  that set up made me more comfortable that a move could be more likely.  Now going forward, whereas in the past i would have primarily relied upon momentum indicators to guide me, i now see this huge positive gamma at 215 and am very doubtful it goes above 215 until after 7/18 (if it does), without another catalyst.

comment.body:
technical indicators are not useful during oscillating trend.
literally no exact answer... in the case of SPX, due to the frequency of the option expos it is more easier to foresee a choppy trend... monitoring macro flows like rotation, etc.
bcs if index vol is sold, beta goes down to, bcs the moves of the baskets annihilate each other.
one can meassure the RoC of the beta to see how it evolves... + take the volatility of the curve to see what counts as "healthy" momentum shift (rough estimation)... also one can see if the mean reverting tendecy is growing or not...

but at the end of the day, there is no free lunch, market cannot be solved.
we only have distribution, certain zones derived from momentum and positioning that reveals us with higher probability, what the market wants. 
but still not 100%, only 'higher' probability

comment.body: I’ve only followed your Substack since probably the beginning of June, didn’t know these chats were available until this week. First just want to give you some well deserved recognition of what you’ve accomplished studying the entirety of the markets… it is hands down one of the most impressive analysis any scholar has achieved at their study… but you make me laugh in a good way about how you fully grasp the factor that nothing in the markets can be considered with a 100 percent certainty you still dedicate you’re time, energy, and skills to its research.. I guess it goes hand and hand with the old saying “in science nothing can be proven certain, only disproven”… I respect your level of knowledge and madness.. it has given myself a new perspective on how I try to understand the whys and the hows of what I’m researching. Thank you.
